headline: Amazon Canopy Bridges Save Lives: Zero Roadkill in 15 Months
meta_description: Eight rope bridges in Brazil's Amazon have enabled 15,000 safe wildlife crossings with zero roadkill deaths, offering hope for fragmented habitats.
summary_short: In Alta Floresta, Brazil, innovative canopy bridges have recorded zero wildlife deaths on roads while enabling thousands of safe animal crossings.
body:
Hours before installing the first bridge in Alta Floresta, biologist Fernanda Abra found two Schneider's marmosets dead on the roadside. Both were endemic Amazon species threatened with extinction. That grim discovery underscored why she had come.

Now, nearly two years later, the outcome tells a different story. Eight canopy bridges strung across the Amazonian town's fragmented forest patches have recorded not a single roadkill among the animals using them. Over 15 months, camera traps documented 15,000 safe crossings by arboreal wildlife—from spider monkeys to critically endangered marmosets.

The rope bridges work by reconnecting divided habitats, allowing animals to move freely while avoiding the deadly ES-164 highway and similar roads that slice through their territories. Each bridge accommodates different species: those with prehensile tails, brachiating spider monkeys, and ground-walking species alike.

Abra, an associate researcher at the Smithsonian Institution and National Geographic Explorer, designed the bridges to be simple, low-cost, and durable. Her success has caught official attention. Brazil's federal transport department has now designated the rope bridge design as the national standard for highways, potentially preventing millions of wildlife deaths annually.

Expansion is underway. Eight more bridges will soon be installed in Alta Floresta's urban area, with insulated power lines added to prevent electrocution. The initiative offers proof that even amid habitat fragmentation, thoughtful intervention can save lives.
